era_context: The late 11th century CE was a period of significant transition in Western Europe, with the Investiture Controversy dominating ecclesiastical politics and the First Crusade preparations underway. The Byzantine Empire under Alexios I Komnenos was seeking Western military assistance against the Seljuks. The Islamic world was fragmented among various dynasties and emirs. East Asia saw the flourishing Song Dynasty and the stable Liao Dynasty. This was a pivotal moment in medieval history, with major geopolitical shifts about to occur.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe and Scandinavia (limited written records)

events.title: Normans capture Palermo from Arab rule, completing major phase of Sicilian conquest
events.description: In 1087, Norman forces under Roger I captured Palermo, the major Arab city in Sicily, marking a crucial milestone in the Norman conquest of the island. The capture of Palermo was a significant military and symbolic victory, as Palermo had been the capital of Arab Sicily. The conquest involved both military siege and negotiation. The fall of Palermo demonstrated Norman military superiority and marked the beginning of the end of Arab rule in Sicily, though the final conquest of the island would not be completed until 1091 with the fall of Noto. The Norman conquest of Sicily had profound implications for Mediterranean politics and Christian-Islamic relations.

sources.name: Geoffrey Malaterra, Deeds of Count Roger

events.certainty_note: The Norman capture of Palermo in 1087 is well-documented in both Norman and Arab sources.
cross_references: 1091-001 (Final Norman conquest of Sicily with fall of Noto)

events.title: Byzantine Emperor Alexios I Komnenos consolidates power and seeks Western military aid
events.description: Emperor Alexios I Komnenos (r. 1081–1118) was consolidating his authority over the Byzantine Empire in 1087. He had come to power in 1081 and was working to restore Byzantine military and political strength after decades of decline. Alexios faced threats from the Seljuk Turks in Anatolia, Norman invaders in the Balkans, and internal political instability. To counter these threats, Alexios was actively seeking military assistance from the West, particularly from the papacy and Western European nobles. His diplomatic overtures would eventually lead to the Byzantine request that prompted the First Crusade. Alexios's reign marked a turning point in Byzantine history, initiating a period of recovery and cultural flourishing known as the Comnenian Renaissance.
events.certainty_note: Alexios I's consolidation of power and diplomatic initiatives are well-documented in Byzantine sources and modern scholarship.

events.title: William the Conqueror dies; succession crisis in Norman England
events.description: William I, the Conqueror, who had invaded England in 1066 and established Norman rule, died on 9 September 1087. His death created a succession crisis that would shape English and Norman history for decades. William's eldest son Robert Curthose inherited Normandy, while his second son William Rufus inherited England. This division of the Norman inheritance between two sons created tension and conflict between the two territories. The succession of William Rufus to the English throne marked the beginning of a new phase in English history, with different rulers controlling England and Normandy. The death of the Conqueror was a major turning point in medieval European history.
events.certainty_note: William the Conqueror's death date and succession are well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources.
cross_references: 1100-001 (Death of William Rufus and succession of Henry I)

events.title: Song Dynasty continues economic and cultural flourishing under Emperor Zhezong
events.description: The Northern Song Dynasty under Emperor Zhezong (r. 1085–1100) was experiencing a period of economic growth, technological innovation, and cultural achievement in 1087. The Song had developed sophisticated systems of printing, ceramics production, and maritime trade. The period saw significant developments in Neo-Confucian philosophy and landscape painting. However, the Song faced persistent military pressure from the Liao Dynasty to the north and the Western Xia kingdom to the northwest, which limited territorial expansion. The Song's economic strength was not matched by military power, creating a strategic vulnerability that would eventually contribute to the dynasty's fall in 1127.

sources.name: Song Shi (History of the Song Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Song Dynasty history is extensively documented in Chinese historical records.
cross_references: 1127-001 (Fall of Northern Song Dynasty to Jurchen Jin)

events.title: Seljuk Sultanate of Rum consolidates control over Anatolia
events.description: The Seljuk Sultanate of Rum, centered in Konya, was consolidating its control over Anatolia in 1087 under Kilij Arslan I (r. 1077–1107). The Seljuks had defeated the Byzantines at Manzikert in 1071 and were now establishing a stable state in Anatolia. The Sultanate of Rum was developing administrative institutions, urban centers, and a mixed Turkish-Greek population. The Seljuk control of Anatolia represented a fundamental shift in the geopolitical balance of the eastern Mediterranean, with Turkish Islamic power replacing Byzantine Christian authority. This Turkish dominance would persist for centuries and would be a major factor in the religious and political tensions that led to the Crusades.
key_figures: Kilij Arslan I (Seljuk Sultan of Rum, r. 1077–1107)

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Seljuk Sultanate of Rum

events.certainty_note: Seljuk consolidation in Anatolia is well-documented in Islamic sources and modern scholarship.

disconfirming_evidence: The entry on William the Conqueror's death is accurate regarding the date (9 September 1087) and the succession of his sons. However, the characterization of the succession as creating a 'crisis' may be somewhat overstated; while there was tension between Robert and William Rufus, the succession itself was orderly and accepted by the Norman nobility. The entry on the Song Dynasty is accurate regarding the general context of economic and cultural flourishing, but the characterization of military pressure from the Liao and Western Xia as 'persistent' may understate the Song's military capabilities and the stability of the frontier. The Song maintained a relatively stable frontier with the Liao through the treaty system established in 1005.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1087 is moderately rich for Western Europe (William the Conqueror's death, Norman consolidation in Sicily, Byzantine diplomatic initiatives) and East Asia (Song Dynasty), but sparse for other regions. The sources are heavily weighted toward elite political and military history. The period is marked by major transitions: the death of the Conqueror, the Norman capture of Palermo, and the consolidation of power by Alexios I Komnenos. These events set the stage for the First Crusade in 1095. Islamic sources are substantial but often focus on internal dynastic politics. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of northern Europe are virtually absent from the surviving written record for this year.

graph_edges.note: Norman capture of Palermo in 1087 led to final conquest of Sicily in 1091
Alexios I's diplomatic initiatives seeking Western military aid prompted the First Crusade
William the Conqueror's death and succession of William Rufus led to succession crisis resolved in 1100
Song Dynasty's economic strength but military vulnerability eventually led to fall in 1127
